tasks.description: Create a single tweet for a simple product announcement
tasks.input: Write a tweet announcing that our open-source CLI tool 'DevFlow' just hit 10,000 GitHub stars. Keep it celebratory and authentic. Include 1-2 relevant hashtags.

scoring.5: Tweet is under 280 characters; concise, punchy, conversational tone; feels native to Twitter culture; celebratory without being corporate
scoring.3: Under 280 characters but reads like a generic announcement; could be from any platform
scoring.1: Exceeds character limit or uses non-Twitter formatting

scoring.5: Includes elements that drive engagement: community gratitude, milestone celebration, implicit CTA (star the repo, celebrate together); emotionally resonant
scoring.3: Decent engagement potential but missing a clear hook or CTA
scoring.1: Flat announcement with no engagement drivers

scoring.5: 1-2 relevant hashtags naturally integrated; tags match the developer/open-source community
scoring.3: Hashtags present but slightly off-target or awkwardly placed
scoring.1: Too many hashtags or irrelevant tags

tasks.description: Write an Instagram caption for a product photo
tasks.input: Write an Instagram caption for a photo of our new ergonomic standing desk 'AltitudeDesk'. The desk features bamboo wood, electric height adjustment, and a built-in wireless charger. Target audience: remote workers and home office enthusiasts. Include relevant hashtags.

scoring.5: Caption is Instagram-native: strong hook in first line, line breaks for readability, 400-800 characters, aspirational/lifestyle tone, complements a visual
scoring.3: Adequate caption but reads like product specs rather than lifestyle content
scoring.1: Too long, too short, or formatted for another platform
scoring.0: Not recognizable as an Instagram caption

scoring.5: First line creates curiosity or emotional pull; ends with a clear CTA (save, comment, share, link in bio); both are platform-appropriate
scoring.3: Hook or CTA present but not both; or both are generic
scoring.1: Weak hook and no CTA
scoring.0: No hook or CTA; reads as product description

scoring.5: 3-5 relevant hashtags; mix of broad (#HomeOffice) and niche (#StandingDeskSetup); placed at caption end
scoring.3: Hashtags present but count or relevance is off
scoring.1: Hashtag spam or irrelevant tags

tasks.description: Create a simple LinkedIn poll post
tasks.input: Create a LinkedIn post with a poll asking engineering managers about their biggest challenge with remote team management. The poll should have 4 options. Include a brief intro that encourages participation and relevant hashtags.

scoring.5: LinkedIn-native tone; professional but conversational; appropriate length for a poll intro (300-600 chars); paragraph breaks for readability
scoring.3: Reasonable LinkedIn post but the poll intro is generic or too formal
scoring.1: Tone mismatch or poor formatting for LinkedIn

scoring.5: 4 clear, distinct, non-overlapping poll options that resonate with engineering managers; options are concise and cover the most common challenges
scoring.3: 4 options but some overlap or are too vague
scoring.1: Options are unclear, overlapping, or not relevant to the audience
scoring.0: No poll options provided or completely irrelevant

scoring.5: Intro asks a thought-provoking question; includes CTA to elaborate in comments; framed to encourage sharing with peers; hashtags are relevant
scoring.3: Basic intro with poll; some engagement element present
scoring.1: Flat intro with no engagement drivers

tasks.description: Adapt a single topic into platform-specific content for two platforms
tasks.input: We just published a blog post titled '5 Lessons from Scaling Our API from 1K to 1M Requests Per Second'. Create social media content to promote it on both Twitter/X (as a thread) and LinkedIn (as a text post). The target audience is backend engineers and engineering leaders. Include platform-appropriate hashtags and timing recommendations.

scoring.5: Twitter thread and LinkedIn post are clearly distinct in tone, format, and length; each feels native to its platform; not a copy-paste between platforms
scoring.3: Some differentiation but the core text is similar; one platform feels more natural than the other
scoring.1: Minimal differentiation; essentially the same content on both platforms

scoring.5: 5-7 tweet thread; tweet 1 is a compelling hook with thread promise; one lesson per tweet; punchy, quotable language; final tweet has summary + CTA + link
scoring.3: Thread structure present but hooks are weak or tweets are too dense
scoring.1: Thread is really just one long tweet split arbitrarily; no narrative flow
scoring.0: Not formatted as a thread or exceeds character limits

scoring.5: 1,200-1,500 chars; professional insights tone; 'see more' hook; lessons presented with leadership context; link in comments note; 3-5 relevant hashtags
scoring.3: Reasonable LinkedIn post but generic hook or missing some elements
scoring.1: Poorly formatted for LinkedIn or reads like a tweet

scoring.5: Platform-specific timing recommendations with rationale; hashtags are different between platforms and appropriate to each; correct count per platform
scoring.3: Some timing/hashtag guidance but not platform-specific
scoring.1: Generic timing or identical hashtags on both platforms

tasks.description: Create a TikTok script with engagement optimization
tasks.input: Write a TikTok video script (30 seconds) for a personal finance creator explaining the '50/30/20 budget rule'. The audience is Gen Z (18-25). The tone should be casual, relatable, and slightly humorous. Include text overlay suggestions, a trending sound recommendation note, and hashtags.

scoring.5: Script is unmistakably TikTok: direct-to-camera, conversational, uses TikTok-native language and formats (POV, 'things nobody tells you', etc.); 21-34 seconds; includes text overlay notes and sound suggestion
scoring.3: Script works for TikTok but could be any short-form video; missing TikTok-specific elements
scoring.1: Script reads like a YouTube explainer or Instagram Reel; not TikTok-native
scoring.0: Not a video script; just written text

scoring.5: First 3 seconds are a powerful hook (pattern interrupt, bold claim, relatable scenario); script maintains momentum throughout; high predicted completion rate
scoring.3: Decent hook but middle section loses momentum; retention might drop
scoring.1: Weak hook; viewer likely swipes away in first 3 seconds
scoring.0: No hook; starts with generic intro

scoring.5: Language, examples, and humor resonate with Gen Z (18-25); uses relatable scenarios (rent, student loans, going out); avoids condescending or overly formal tone
scoring.3: Generally appropriate but tone feels slightly off for Gen Z
scoring.1: Tone is too formal, too corporate, or talks down to the audience

scoring.5: Includes specific text overlay suggestions per scene, trending sound/audio recommendation, hashtags (3-5, niche + trending), and CTA (follow, comment, save)
scoring.3: Some production notes present but incomplete
scoring.1: Minimal or no production notes

tasks.description: Create an Instagram carousel concept with strategic hashtags
tasks.input: Design an Instagram carousel (8-10 slides) for a SaaS startup's account on the topic '7 Signs Your Startup Needs to Invest in DevOps'. Target audience: startup CTOs and technical co-founders. Provide the text content for each slide, hashtag strategy, and posting time recommendation.

scoring.5: 8-10 slides with clear structure: cover slide (bold hook), context slide, 7 sign slides (one per slide, concise), CTA slide; each slide has one focused takeaway; readable at mobile scale
scoring.3: Slide count and structure are acceptable but some slides are too dense or structure is unclear
scoring.1: Poor structure; multiple points per slide; no clear flow
scoring.0: Not a carousel format; just a text list

scoring.5: Each sign is specific, actionable, and resonates with startup CTOs; uses concrete examples or scenarios; builds from obvious to non-obvious signs; professional but engaging tone
scoring.3: Content is relevant but generic; signs could apply to any company, not startup-specific
scoring.1: Vague or irrelevant points; doesn't demonstrate DevOps expertise

scoring.5: Includes text formatting suggestions (font size, emphasis), slide layout notes, and brand consistency guidance; content is designed for visual consumption
scoring.3: Some visual notes but minimal; content relies heavily on text
scoring.1: No visual direction; pure text content
scoring.0: Visual suggestions are inappropriate for Instagram

scoring.5: 3-5 hashtags following relevance pyramid (mix of broad #StartupLife and niche #DevOpsCulture); posting time with timezone and day-of-week recommendation; rationale provided
scoring.3: Hashtags and timing present but generic or not optimized
scoring.1: Missing hashtags or timing recommendation

tasks.description: Create content with engagement prediction and A/B variant
tasks.input: Create a Twitter/X tweet promoting a free webinar on 'How to Land Your First Developer Advocate Role'. Target audience: developers interested in DevRel. Provide the main tweet, an A/B test variant with a different hook, engagement prediction scores for both, and timing recommendation. Include hashtags.

scoring.5: Both tweet variants are under 280 chars; different hooks (e.g., one question-based, one data-driven); both feel natural and compelling; proper Twitter formatting
scoring.3: Two variants provided but hooks are too similar or one feels forced
scoring.1: Only one tweet or variants are nearly identical
scoring.0: Tweets exceed character limit or are poorly formatted

scoring.5: Scores both variants on hook strength, value delivery, format alignment, CTA clarity, and algorithmic fit (1-5 each); explains scoring rationale; predicts which variant will perform better and why
scoring.3: Some scoring provided but incomplete dimensions or missing rationale
scoring.1: Vague prediction without structured scoring

scoring.5: Content speaks directly to developers curious about DevRel; uses language and pain points familiar to this audience (coding + communication, community building, career transition)
scoring.3: Generally relevant but could target any career webinar
scoring.1: Doesn't speak to the developer-to-DevRel audience specifically

scoring.5: 1-2 relevant hashtags per variant (DevRel, developer community); specific timing recommendation with timezone and rationale
scoring.3: Hashtags and timing present but not optimized for the developer audience
scoring.1: Generic or missing

tasks.description: Full multi-platform content strategy for a product launch
tasks.input: We're launching a new AI code review tool called 'ReviewBot' next Tuesday. Create a coordinated launch day content plan across all four platforms (Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Instagram, TikTok). For each platform, provide: the full post/script content, hashtags, exact posting time with timezone, and media suggestions. The target audience is software engineering teams at mid-size companies (50-500 employees). Our key differentiators: 1) Reviews PRs in under 60 seconds, 2) Learns team-specific coding standards, 3) Reduces code review bottlenecks by 70%. The tone should be confident but not hype-driven.

scoring.5: All 4 platforms covered with clearly differentiated content; posting times are staggered strategically throughout launch day; each platform leverages its unique strengths; coherent narrative across platforms without redundancy
scoring.3: All 4 platforms covered but content feels repetitive; timing is not strategically staggered
scoring.1: Only 2-3 platforms covered or content is essentially the same across platforms
scoring.0: Only 1 platform or cross-posted identical content

scoring.5: Each platform's content is native: Twitter thread with quotable stats, LinkedIn thought-leadership post, Instagram visual-first carousel concept, TikTok demo-style script; all within platform limits; strong hooks on every platform
scoring.3: Content is adequate but not optimized for each platform's native format
scoring.1: Content feels generic across platforms; not leveraging platform-specific formats
scoring.0: Content violates platform norms or exceeds limits

scoring.5: Clear posting schedule with specific times per platform; considers timezone coverage for global audience; accounts for algorithm peak times; sequence creates momentum throughout the day
scoring.3: Timing provided but not strategically sequenced
scoring.1: Vague timing or all posts at the same time

scoring.5: All 3 differentiators are communicated across the campaign; each platform highlights different angles; data points (60 sec, 70% reduction) are used effectively without feeling repetitive; tone is confident but grounded
scoring.3: Key differentiators mentioned but not strategically distributed; some repetition
scoring.1: Only 1-2 differentiators covered; feels like a generic product launch

tasks.description: Create a content series plan with audience growth strategy
tasks.input: Design a 5-part LinkedIn content series called 'The Uncomfortable Truths About Engineering Management' for an engineering leader building their personal brand. The series should be posted over 5 consecutive weekdays (Monday-Friday). For each installment, provide: the full post text (1,200-1,500 chars), hashtags, posting time, and explain how each post builds on the previous one. Each post should end with a teaser for the next day's post. Also provide an engagement prediction for the overall series and explain why this series format drives follower growth.

scoring.5: 5 posts form a clear narrative arc; each post builds on the previous; teasers create anticipation; topics progress from accessible to deep; series title and numbering are consistent
scoring.3: 5 posts on related topics but weak connective tissue between them; teasers feel forced
scoring.1: Posts are standalone with no series feel; could be published in any order
scoring.0: Posts are unrelated or series structure is absent

scoring.5: Each post is 1,200-1,500 chars; strong hook; single-sentence paragraphs; personal/vulnerable tone matching 'uncomfortable truths' theme; specific examples not generic advice; CTAs that drive comments
scoring.3: Posts are adequate LinkedIn content but don't fully deliver on the 'uncomfortable truths' promise
scoring.1: Posts are generic management advice without the edge the series title promises
scoring.0: Posts are poorly formatted or violate LinkedIn norms

scoring.5: Explains why the series format drives follower growth (anticipation, returning viewers, algorithm rewards consistency); engagement prediction with scoring breakdown for the series; identifies which post will likely perform best and why
scoring.3: Some growth strategy explanation but lacks depth or specificity
scoring.1: Minimal strategy explanation; no engagement prediction

scoring.5: Specific posting times for each day (Mon-Fri) with timezone; times vary slightly based on content type; 3-5 consistent but topic-adapted hashtags per post; mix of series-branded and topic tags
scoring.3: Timing and hashtags provided but identical across all posts or not optimized
scoring.1: Vague timing; inconsistent or missing hashtags

tasks.description: Adapt sensitive/complex topic for social media with tone management
tasks.input: A cybersecurity company needs to post about a major data breach affecting 50 million users of a competitor's platform. Create content for Twitter/X (a thread explaining the breach and lessons learned) and LinkedIn (a thought-leadership post on what this means for the industry). The tone must be: informative and authoritative, NOT opportunistic or fear-mongering; empathetic toward affected users; educational about prevention without being preachy; subtly positioning the company as knowledgeable without exploiting the situation. Include hashtags, timing, and engagement predictions. Note any content that should be avoided.

scoring.5: Tone is precisely calibrated: authoritative but empathetic; educational without being preachy; positions expertise without exploiting the breach; acknowledges affected users; avoids schadenfreude or competitive jabs
scoring.3: Generally appropriate tone but slips into mild opportunism or fear-mongering in places
scoring.1: Tone is off — either too promotional, too alarmist, or insensitive
scoring.0: Tone is exploitative, fear-mongering, or clearly self-serving

scoring.5: Thread and post provide genuine educational value; explain the breach clearly; offer actionable prevention lessons; include what-to-avoid guidance; both pieces stand alone as valuable content
scoring.3: Content is informative but surface-level; prevention advice is generic
scoring.1: Content is primarily about the company, not education
scoring.0: Content is misleading, inaccurate, or purely promotional

scoring.5: Twitter thread is concise, factual, and structured for quick consumption; LinkedIn post is deeper, more reflective, with industry-level analysis; each leverages platform strengths for the sensitive topic
scoring.3: Some differentiation but both platforms cover the same ground in similar ways
scoring.1: Minimal differentiation; content is interchangeable

scoring.5: Explicitly notes content to avoid (naming specific victims, speculating on attack vectors without evidence, using breach images, competitive attacks); timing recommendation accounts for news cycle sensitivity; hashtags avoid trending exploitation tags
scoring.3: Some risk awareness but incomplete; misses some potential pitfalls
scoring.1: Minimal risk awareness; content could backfire
scoring.0: No risk awareness; content is reckless
